payload.text: I want an infinite bird guessing game app. Use A2:A95 in the attached spreadsheet for the list of birds to include. Use the corresponding cells F2:I95 for the pictures. I want it to randomly choose one picture and ask for what bird it is. The app provides 1 correct option and 3 incorrect options. The incorrect options are birds with similar traits (for example, if the bird is yellow, the 3 incorrect options are also yellow. If the bird is a duck, the 3 incorrect options are other ducks from the list.)

**Defining the Data Structure**

I'm now focusing on structuring the bird data for the guessing game. I've pinpointed the relevant columns in the CSV: bird names are in column A, and the picture URLs are in columns F through I. I'm prioritizing rows A2 through A95 initially. The next step is to figure out the best way to handle the picture URLs.


**Refining the Trait Strategy**

I've decided to refine the approach for choosing incorrect options by leveraging the Gemini API to categorize birds into groups or traits at the outset. This will enrich the data and ensure that incorrect options share similar characteristics with the correct bird. To optimize, I might hardcode a trait map if the list is fixed.


**Outlining the Project Structure**

I'm now outlining the project's architecture, and I will be using React 18+, Tailwind CSS, Lucide React icons, and Framer Motion for smooth animations. I'm focusing on defining core components and file structure now. I'm also preparing for the CSV parsing and trait grouping, which will either be manual or programmatically.


**Defining Game Logic**

I'm now focusing on the game logic. The plan is: select a random bird and one of its pictures, then determine its group. Based on that group, I'll select three other birds from the same group. If the group is small, I will default to a broader group, or similar names. I am also working on the aesthetics, clean with nature-inspired visuals. Visual feedback for correct and incorrect answers and a "Next" or auto-advance feature is in the works. My CSV data will include species like California Thrasher, Northern Mockingbird, etc. My grouping strategy now includes Mimids, Icterids, and other groups.


**Refining the Categorization**

I'm now refining the bird categorization strategy. My existing grouping strategy (Mimids, Icterids, etc.) will be supplemented with a manual categorizer based on name keywords to avoid relying solely on the Gemini API. To ensure perfect "similar traits" options, I'll leverage Gemini to create a similarity matrix for the 94 birds, and use this data for a snappy, well-informed game.

entries.description: App metadata with name and description.
Primary entry point served in the browser.
Parsing and cleaning the provided bird dataset.

diffs.replacement:

entries.description: Helper functions for managing the game flow and similar bird selection.
Main application component managing game state and rendering.

payload.text: Make sure the pictures do not get cropped.

**Analyzing Image Display**

I've been examining the image rendering in `App.tsx`, and it looks like `object-cover` is being used. My key concern is how this affects the bird images. I must consider how this style might lead to unwanted cropping. I need to figure out a better solution.


**Refining Image Presentation**

I've decided that `object-contain` is the better approach in `App.tsx` because it keeps the bird images complete. I'm going to update the code to use it instead of `object-cover`. This will make them fully visible and prevent cropping, even if that means the background shows. I'm also preparing for the visual appearance to be better if the container is not the exact size needed. My next step is reviewing the code for other image display instances.

entries.description: Changed bird image rendering from object-cover to object-contain to ensure images are never cropped.
